speaker: interviewer
text: --about your departure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So there was this curfew. We all had rucksacks. You could pack in your rucksack whatever you could take with you. They told you to bring your valuables with you. Did you pack your own rucksack?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother, my mother. We bought new rucksacks, you know, that-- and big rucksacks, the biggest that, that a child could carry. And Zlata came with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the redhead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now she didn't have papers. And she was in Valasske Mezirici unofficially. And she could have stayed, you know, without papers. Somebody would have hidden her. We had Czech friends. But she went to Theresienstadt, talked to people, and she was told, we are interned here. In the ghetto, there are no beatings. It's-- we're completely self-contained here. We're a bit hungry. But it's not too bad. So that message that she brought back, my mother communicated to the community. What did you understand, at that time, about going to Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We were just-- I thought it was an adventure. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we went-- we were all assembled. We had to go to this siding. We were told where to go. Streets were completely deserted. [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] And we walked past some Jewish houses, people that I knew. The [? Takhs, ?] who were friends of ours-- twins-- the [? Takh ?] brothers. We played violin together. We had the same violin teacher. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And sort of out of the darkness came Jews with rucksacks walking to this siding behind the park. [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] So the Czechs could see the Jews were there one day, and the next day, the Jews were gone. [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] That was the way that the Germans had designed it. And they did it all over Czechoslovakia in exactly the same way.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you talked to the other boys in Theresienstadt. And they would tell you the same story. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now we went to [? Moravska Ostrava. ?] We were put in a school, which was empty at the time. It was the beginning of September. So there were no Czech children at school. And the whole school was full of people. And it was fenced. And we stayed there a number of days. And people kept, kept arriving from various villages and other small towns in the district. And then, one day, we marched out. And again, curfew. Now, [? Moravska Ostrava, ?] or Ostrava, is a big town. There was a town curfew. And we walked through this curfew to a siding a long way from this school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] we got separated from my mother, because my number was 85. My brother was 84. And my mother was 86. And Zlata was 87.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother and Zlata went in one particular carriage. And we went in another carriage. These were still personal carriages. You know, they still had seats.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other-- any other family members with you? No, [? Melosh ?] and I, and mother and Zlata.

speaker: interviewer
text: Grandparents, aunts, uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- all my grandparents had died before the war. Uncles were all in Poland. We were a, a self-contained family. Two brothers were in Palestine by then. One brother was in England with my father, Henry. My father was in England. So there was my mother. Leo had gone a year earlier to Theresienstadt with the original two transports that were the, the builders of the ghetto. And the agreement was that [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] the families of these people who would get the ghetto going would be protected by the family members who went there. And there were something like 200 in the first two transports. They were all young men. My brother was a doctor.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he immediately became a doctor in the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] And that's, in fact, how my brother Leo saved my life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But that's a different story. Is it an appropriate place to tell me now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I need to tell you how we arrived, because I told you how [? Melosh ?] was supplanted by me as the youngest child. And his problem was that he was jealous of me. I mean, he was a very loving boy. But we had a problem because-- it was his problem, not mine. But it was his problem that-- but as he grew older, and as we became more and more alone with my mother as the years came to 1944, our relationship as brothers strengthened, because he started working on a Jewish farm. [? Livingshtine's ?] were the farmers that he worked with.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know I gave you the picture? [? Livingshtine. ?] Funny how it comes to you when you, when you talk about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as he was-- he worked, first of all, at the, at the furniture factory, to be an apprentice carpenter. And then, they were afraid of employing a Jew. So he went and worked with the [? Livingshtines. ?] They were the only farmers, Jewish farmers, in the town. Quite picturesque. I mean, you plowed with horses. And you sowed by hand. And it was all very primitive, but very-- it was a beautiful life. Well, beautiful, [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] you know, women had to wash up and forever feed the cows, and feed the horses. And there was a drudgery. But to a kid, a farm is, is terrifically exciting, and animals. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The [? Livingshtines ?] weren't kosher. They had rabbits. And they ate rabbits.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were saying in Theresienstadt with [? Melosh. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: So my relationship with [? Melosh, ?] or his problem disappeared. He became 16. I was 13. So as he was 16, and he was now the-- he felt himself beginning to be a man. [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] So I was very much the younger brother. And he very much would look after me. And he was a lovely, lovely man, lovely boy. Many of my brothers were terrific guys. I mean, I loved them. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I have to compose myself a bit. The first time that I discovered that I really knew that [? Melosh ?] loved me was when we walked from the station in Theresienstadt to Theresienstadt. The station was in a station called Bohusovice, which is a village outside Theresienstadt. And actually, during the year that we were in Theresienstadt, the Germans had us, the Jews, build an extension line right into the ghetto so that they could privately send transports from the ghetto and the Jews wouldn't have to march through this countryside. And the station was called Bohusovice. [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we come to Bohusovice, and somebody already says, you're family of Leo Honigswachs, you know, he's waiting for you. He knows you're coming. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he will see you in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the train, we were-- [? Melosh ?] and I were separate from my mother. In the train, I sort of went up to see if I can find my mother. And of course, my mother was looking after the elderly. And you know, there was this person crying and that person crying. My mother was busy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came. [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] And I sort of said, we are all right. You know, we're three carriages away. And she said, fine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And you see, that was the first that I saw that my mother was beginning to regard us both as independent of her, that she had other things to worry about, and I wasn't the priority, suddenly. That sort of growing up, you know, that sort of realization. The other realization, we, we march evening, towards evening. It gets dark. I had this huge rucksack. And it's about a mile and a half from Bohusovice to Theresienstadt. And I sort of stumbled. And [? Melosh ?] said, it's not much longer, you know. Keep it up. And from the tone of his voice, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I could see he loved me. So the scraps that we had, you know, when we were much smaller children, and when I knew he was jealous of me, were forgotten, like in a-- like it's, it's different, suddenly. Anyway, we get into the camp. And Leo takes us out of the quarantine. Oh, you go through something called [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] in Theresienstadt was where you were supposed to be deloused. I mean, we came clean, you know, from, from home. So you're supposed to be deloused. So you have to get completely undressed. And everything is examined. And all the valuables that we were told to bring with us are taken off you. So it's, it's robbery, you see, the, the-- you see that the internment is, in fact, robbery, because any money, everything is-- but everything that's confiscated is written down, you know, as if you're going to get it after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did you have in your-- what did you have in your rucksack?

words.text: else did you have in your-- what did

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we did have money. And we did have some [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] diamonds and all kinds of things. Everybody did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, clothes, spare clothes. You know, linen. All kinds of things. Blanket, the blanket around. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Things that you need for camping. Actually, we were very good, [? Melosh ?] and I, for equipment. You know, how to equip a list to go on an extended stay, because we had been to these Maccabi Hatzair camps, and we had packed rucksacks. I mean, I knew how to roll up everything in tiny little rows so that you, you get as much as possible into the rucksack, which I was taught camping, you see. [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] I mean, camping. I was taught how to find the north star, so that you could find where you are, you know. That's very important, that-- later on, it  became terrifically important to orientate oneself, because that's all you had, the north star.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else do you remember about the delousing process?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we all got dressed. We had a shower. We all got dressed. It wasn't like Auschwitz. That's a different delousing. There, it was called the [NON-ENGLISH]. And here, it was called the [NON-ENGLISH], which is supposed to be the Czech word for it. [NON-ENGLISH] means [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] a place which is closed. Anyway, we get into the quarantine. And we are in quarantine because three days later, the whole transport is going off to the east. But Leo is taking us out. And he says, come on, we're going. Pack your things. We're going. And my mother says, there's Zlata here. She's coming with us. And Leo says, I don't have a permit for her. I only have entitlement to protect my family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He had married [INAUDIBLE] by this time. And he was married. So my mother said-- well, my mother actually said, marry her. So they know here that I'm here with my wife. She's already here, and I'm married. There's no way that I'll get permission for, for a stranger because it's-- I don't run this, this city. You know, I'm just one of the-- anyway, my mother sends him packing. And he tries and comes back. And he actually has to have a word with Zlata to convince her that my mother and us two should stay in Theresienstadt and not go with her to the east. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the two women part. It's quite a horrendous parting, because my mother knows that you know, Zlata is going back to Poland because of-- because of her opinion and her advice. I think my mother must have realized, for the first time, that she made a mistake.

speaker: interviewer
text: In?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she made a mistake. She didn't go to England to follow my father. She must have realized earlier, as well. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the full realization probably didn't hit her until Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or I wonder if it ever hit her, because can a woman live with that, you know, that she had the money, and the means, and the papers, and the advice? And for reasons, which were probably to do with her change or something, something, a woman's mind [PAUSES FOR 1 SECOND] after six children and after, I don't know, 35 years of marriage or something? A woman suddenly becomes-- thinks she's independent, you know. She's the judge.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, my mother just wasn't up to it, because as a judge of circumstances, she was duped. [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] When I was in the camps and I saw that-- you know, I was in on it when we sent the lift with all the furniture and all our stuff. And we were, like, going to go get on a train and go to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939. In 19-- yeah, in 1939. And then, we didn't go, for reasons that my mother never explained. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I mean, you couldn't discuss that with my mother, because children were-- [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] you know, children are seen but not heard. And things are not discussed with young children. My mother wouldn't even discuss things with Leo. She wouldn't obey him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My brother Henry went illegally, after the Germans occupied Czechoslovakia, across the border into Poland. Poland wasn't attacked yet. And he left with the last ship out of Poland to England. And he [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] had an arrangement with Leo that the next night, Leo would put us two on the same train. It was a wagons train. And there was a [? rail-woman ?] who would take us across the border. And he phoned, and he said, it's all right. I'm here. Do it. [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] Now, Henry was saying to his elder brother, Leo, to kidnap me and, and [? Melosh ?], and take us across the border to Poland so that he would then pick us up and organize us to all go too. And then, my mother would come on the third night with Leo, because once the children would be gone, Henry said she would follow the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: But Leo wasn't like Henry. Leo wouldn't disobey Mom. [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] So [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] when Henry was dying, poor chap, a year and a half ago, the last two weeks, he was talking about nothing else. Just about this, that why didn't Leo kidnap us? You know, because we would have saved the whole family. Because Henry felt, you know, that he saved himself, because he went. He was the first one.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then Leo said, well, I'm not coming. Leo said I'm not coming, because he told Mother I'll take the children. And Mother said, no, you won't, obviously. I mean, I wasn't there at that discussion. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So my mother-- you know, there were lots of events that took place, which I wasn't privy to, but which I know happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened at Theresienstadt with Leo and Zlata and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Zlata went east. And that's the last we saw of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you understand, at that time, what 'going east' meant?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, just more difficult camps than Theresienstadt. That's all we-- That's all we knew. I was taken to a Jugendheim. And who, who should be running the Jugendheim and the [NON-ENGLISH] but Freddy Hirsch? Freddy Hirsch was on the [NON-ENGLISH] as [NON-ENGLISH], youth elder. The [NON-ENGLISH] means the elders of the city were running. [? Edelstein, ?] who was the elder of the Jews was a [NON-ENGLISH] Maccabi Hatzair of Leo's. So Leo went on the AK transport with [? Edelstein, ?] because [? Edelstein ?] made this crazy agreement with, with, with the Germans, you know, that they would open the ghetto, and that because of that, they could protect their families. And there was this thing that arose in Theresienstadt called Proteczia. I don't know if you remember a book called by Heller.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me more about the Proteczia in Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Proteczia. Well it's like

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to have-- you had to have Proteczia. And there were different degrees of Proteczia. I mean, people who were working on the [NON-ENGLISH] had the top Proteczia. There was a man who was repairing watches for the SS, a watchmaker. He happened to be the father of one of the boys who, who, with whom I lived in Theresienstadt. He had top Proteczia. You know, don't send him. He's repairing our watches. [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] What he was doing, he was repairing any watches which were confiscated in the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, in order to make them into marketable goods.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was watchmaker. Doctors had Proteczia. My brother, Leo, had top Proteczia because he was an AK transport, one of the pioneers to open up the ghetto, and he was a doctor, as well. So he was protected from-- and he gave us protection. So we had sort of second-rate Proteczia, because we had Proteczia via him. How did that work in everyday situations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when you were given jobs because of Proteczia. If you were a young man, a job which would be desirable would be in the kitchens.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't have a job. I was a kid of 13. But [? Melosh ?] went to work in the kitchens, because Leo had Proteczia. So [? Melosh ?] had Proteczia through Leo. And this Proteczia worked right through the ghetto. It's a pernicious thing, really, when you think about it. And the communists utilized it for anti-Jewish propaganda, anti-Zionist propaganda, mainly because the Zionists instigated it. But it's the sort of thing that operates in prisons, you see. And Theresienstadt was a prison. So the same mores that happened in prisons happened in Theresienstadt. You have to make do. You know, you have to survive. And people survive by treading on others. That's how you survive. [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you, you learn this lesson as a kid of 13. And you see it happening everywhere, because this is the law of Proteczia. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We were giving-- given lessons in this Jugendheim where I was. The Jugendheim was like a public school, terrific. We had terrific teachers. [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] Unlike, unlike l'cheder, you know, there were people who were really learning Hebrew. I wanted to learn a trade. And there was no way of organizing that I should learn a trade, even with Proteczia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you learn there?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we learned grammar, history, [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] all kinds of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the main language of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Czech. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Although, Freddy never spoke Czech. He spoke German. I mean, everybody spoke German. But the lingua franca was Czech, because this was Czech Jewry. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we had a [INAUDIBLE]. We were the Zionist room, number five, [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] means freedom. It also means sparrow. It's a funny thing. The [NON-ENGLISH] is a little bird, you see, sparrow. But when somebody looked it up in a, in a dictionary of Hebrew, the dictionary said 'swallow.'

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were called The Swallows in Czech. And when the Germans asked about, you know, our football team, we had an emblem of the swallow. We are The Swallows. But we knew it was freedom. So there was this sort of undercurrent that we were in a prison.

speaker: interviewee
text: But [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] the objective of the teaching, and of the whole games that were arranged-- football, sport and so on-- [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] the whole policy was to remove the children from the prison atmosphere, and to create an atmosphere in this school as if it's a public school. You know, you're, you're boarding in a public school. And it was very formative for many of us, including me. We think of it fondly, and the girls, as well. You know, there were two girls' schools like this. [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] You talked to the girls of these different Jugendheims, and you talk to the children who were not in the Jugendheims, who were with their parents. They were very much hard done by, like Harry Lowett, or Helmut Spritzer. They weren't in the Jugendheim. Harry Lowett because he was, somehow-- he couldn't leave his mother. His mother got hysterical or something I don't know how. But there were, there were cases, exceptions. But mainly, the Jugendheim was the place to be, because it was-- the other children that weren't in the Jugendheim envied us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have day-to-day contact with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, every day. Every evening, we met as a family. [PAUSES FOR 1 SECOND] Leo, my brother [? Melosh ?], my aunt, my sister-in-law [INAUDIBLE], and my mother. Now Proteczia was one thing. Stealing was something else. Stealing was called organization. It wasn't called stealing. If you went somewhere, to a store, and you stole, and you stole potatoes, particularly if the store was outside the walls, that was organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your personal involvement in organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I once went on an expedition like this, out of the walls, and brought back two pounds of potatoes. And Leo said to me, you absolutely must not go the next time. I forbid it, because two pounds of potatoes isn't worth a life. If the Germans catch you, they'll shoot you. They'll beat you to death, in fact. By the time that we arrived, you couldn't see a German soldier inside the ghetto. When the ghetto started, [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] the Germans had to convert these, these young men, who were all sorts of independent-minded young men, you know, who knew how to organize. And stealing, sending mail, you know, doing anything which was illegal, they would do.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you discipline such a Jewish rabble? [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] You know, how do you discipline such Jewish people? You take two of them. Two, two postcards were intercepted. So the Germans say, who sent the postcards? [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] And-- and there will be no punishment. Who sent the postcards? Postcards are not allowed. We just want to know who sent the postcards. We know the people who received them. If the people don't come forward, we will take the people who received them, and we will bring them here, and they will tell us who sent the postcard. Will the two men who sent the postcards step out of line?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two men stepped out of line. They're put into solitary confinement, in the ghetto still, not in the-- the ghetto wasn't yet the whole town. It was just one of-- one barracks. There are these big barrack buildings in Theresienstadt, which are from the time of Maria Theresa. I don't know if you know the history of it. It's quite interesting. And they're quite imposing buildings, these, these barracks. They're not like barracks, you know, wooden huts, little things. They are big, three-storied, catacomb of buildings. And in the middle of the buildings, there's a big playing field, a football field is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we stop the tape there?
